source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 9.2 Viewing processes (pp. 158-159)

questions.q: Display ALL processes on the system in full format.
questions.why: -e shows every process and -f gives the full-format listing.

questions.q: What does the -e option of ps do?
options: Displays only the current user's processes
questions.why: -e shows all processes; -f is full format, -H is the tree view.

questions.q: Display the processes belonging to the user root, in full format.
questions.why: -u <login> restricts the listing to that user's processes; -f adds the full format.

questions.q: With no options, what does ps display?
options: Only the processes running from the current terminal
Nothing until a PID is given
questions.why: Unqualified, ps shows only processes from the current terminal.

questions.q: Display the information for the single process with PID 1664.
questions.why: -p <PID> shows information for that specific process.

questions.q: In 'ps -ef' output, what does the PPID column show?
questions.why: PPID is the parent process ID; C is priority, TIME is duration, TTY is the terminal, CMD the command.

questions.q: Which ps option shows the processes in a tree structure?
questions.why: -H prints the process hierarchy as a tree; -l is long format.
